title: Appealing company from the outside!
pros: Friendly co-workers Flexible work arrangements
cons: Often overworked, worked multiple weekends, 10-12 hr shifts, overnight, traveling for work and at the end of the day they still had the audacity to put me on a PIP. Iâm not entirely sure what their bottom line for âacceptable performanceâ is. The day I was put on one I dusted off my resume and got out of there ASAP. Organizational restructuring happened during my time there that gave other groups more work on top of their pre-existing roles without raises. And all in all, there was about 5-6 other co-workers in my overarching group alone that had left the company entirely in 2025 due to poor pay and being overworked. Heard through other workers that people had also left the company quite often, from shop floor workers to senior management positions.

title: Stellar Company Culture & Multi-Generational Employees
pros: Having worked at everything from startup to Fortune 20s, I have seen the disasters and chaos so I appreciate coming to a place that treats people with respect, is fun, and doesn't set unreal expectations. I tell my friends this is the most unique company culture I have had the pleasure to see. I understand why people work here generationally and those that seem to not enjoy it tend to be people that have worked here since college so probably good growth to see what is outside of Siemens. They aim towards salaries that are very competitive, great benefits including healthcare, 401K, time off...they actually /truly/ understand work/life balance.
cons: Is it perfect, no. Does it have to be, no. Mega company so certainly challenges with different divisions so certainly acknowledge that experiences may differ depending who the leader is or where in the world people work. Downsides for me is that there are silos both divisionally and geographically. It's hard to know everyone you need to know in a 300K+ person company. It's also really hard to get into this company as there are many applicants. I have tried to refer really qualified previous qualified colleagues and it is really competitive to join because people tend to stay here for decades. Double edged sword. For those trying, patience and diligence. Network as much as possible.

title: Avoid this place. Don't waste your time.
body: Avoid this place. Don't waste your time.
pros: - Good work life balance. - Flexibility in remote working.
cons: - No scope for career growth. - Very focused on billing hours. - The job description is deceiving. They say Research Scientist but you hardly get to do any research. The focus was always on procuring customers and trying to bill them as much as possible with easy solution that rarely involve any actual research work. - Will lay you off brutally. - Compensation and benefits are below industry standards. - The longer you stay here, the more unemployable you will become outside this company as there is no scope for technical skills development. - Also, very partial to Germans. Other nationalities are not given priority for promotions and given priority for lay-offs. But they don't lay off Germans.

title: Great Company
pros: Discretionary PTO with GBS, and support from leadership to take a large portion of time off every year. Great benefits - for ex. 4 dental cleanings per year instead of the standard 2. Employee share matching. 401k matching by 6% etc. Remote work is encouraged. Leadership is supportive and great to work with. Looking for workers to grow by encouraging them to use internal learning platforms, some departments even incentivize learning hours. It is also free to use platforms like corsera, and linkedin learning to advance your career through the professional courses they offer.
cons: Some internal processes are time consuming with lots of redundant work.

title: Diverse and modern company to work for!
body: Diverse and modern company to work for!
pros: Diverse company with lots of options to move horizontally and vertically. As a commercial project manager, there are plenty of options to work remote, in office, or hybrid. Amazing opportunities for growth and development.
cons: May or may not be a con, but If youâre a younger professional, you will typically be on teams with or working cross departments with more tenured employees. Also, with Siemens being such a large company, lots of moving pieces and changes across the business can be quite stressful and can feel a bit disorganized in certain business units.

title: No career advancement
pros: Global market leader in industrial automation
cons: Professional success seems contingent on personal relationship rather than merit! Team members witnessing consistent preferential treatment leading to disheartened and affected feature motivation. Skilled professionals potentially leaving the company due to frustration over perceived nepotism in promotions. Projects suffering due to the assignment of roles and the position based on connections rather than competence. Demotivation and discontent experienced by non-favored employees who witness preferential treatment! When promotions seem influenced more by similar backgrounds than by merit, trust in leader diminishes, hindering the open communication necessary for a vibrant workplace! Frustration and dissatisfaction caused by perceived unfairness can drive talented employees to seek opportunities elsewhere, leading to higher turnover rates and subsequent decline in overall employee engagement!

All the work got outsourced to a offshore teams in India while the company changing it's product strategy to make data API the top priority.

title: Good Paycheck, but no fun energy
body: Good Paycheck, but no fun energy
pros: They got me a job as a junior developer, which I am very grateful for. They coincidentally had just brought someone in to train the team on the software program we were using when I first got there. Unfortunately, I didn't have a good frame of reference to understand what to ask because I had never used the software. It turned out that using the software wasn't much of a problem anyway, but I appreciated being introduced to it in that way. To help get me up to speed I would pair program with the worker next to me a bit each day for a least a month. We also went to an event where someone talked about the stuff they learned taking a course from the company, so they do have some resources and interest in growing the employees.
cons: The code was mostly written in a hacky scripting language which isn't used outside of this project. Besides making the software harder to write and therefore also harder to read, it also meant you weren't gaining experience in a particular language which can be helpful in getting the next job. The work was late-stage bugs where you were digging through log files trying to figure out what happened. As it goes with late-stage bugs, the bugs were more difficult to track down. Although I really enjoy coding I found the experience of working there low energy. It seemed like just another office job with no energy or excitement.

title: Great program to start my career. Bad options to roll out into a full time role.
body: Great program to start my career. Bad options to roll out into a full time role.
pros: Siemens was a great place to work. It is a massive company with an infinite amount of promotion potential and a great culture to match. The people who work there were all very intelligent and very motivated. All of the teams that I worked with at Siemens were great.
cons: For the engineering leadership development program, they preach that you have the ability to make a role when you roll off of the program. Unfortunately, you only get offered the open positions. If those open positions are bad, you don't really get other options. Also, because Siemens is a distributor, you don't do a lot of hand-on engineering work. Mainly, you discuss the products instead of using them.
role: Former employee - Engineering Leadership Development Program

title: Great place if you are willing to learn
body: Great place if you are willing to learn
pros: - ops treats their technicians good. understanding. lenient. ask for work or OT and you shall receive - Its okay to ask for PTO, they understand your a human being. - siemens have their own training center for basic and advanced training. - work van, paid gas and maintenance, phone and laptop - helpful tip: all automation companies are dumpster fires when it comes to projects. submittals/ engineering will be messed up, programming will be messed up, customer will be unhappy to hear about it and it halting progress.... but thats honestly the job you get paid to do is to fix it all. so if you are okay instantly combusting into flames and say "this is fine" you be okay. if not then not the right industry for you. -comparing to other automation companies siemens has been the best for me
cons: - management being beat down by big siemens pending on project status, which means for work and stress for you on the bottom to keep on track with projects. - not enough experienced technicians to share the knowledge. you will have to learn by yourself and then help others learn. some people can do it, others cant.

title: Good company, Great products, Good benefits, but poor management
body: Good company, Great products, Good benefits, but poor management
pros: + Competitive salaries for skilled positions, depending on work experience + Nice working environment with work/life balance + ENG Design and in-house manufacturing at the same place + Job security with 5-10 year backlogs of customer orders + Travel job opportunity as a German worldwide company
cons: + Not prioritize product quality, but manufacturing speed + Poor management that does not invest/care about employee's career development. There's almost no career level structures for most positions. The only way to get a wage increase is changing job titles by switching team/dept which leads to knowledge drain and company un-stability. + Un-organized production floor, warehouse, walk-ways, storage across the facilities (sometimes considered as war zone) due to lacking of process, poor ownership/responsibility, and coordination. + Very poor training, coaching, performance review which create in-competent staff across multiple departments

title: Pay compensation not good with work
body: Pay compensation not good with work
pros: Working at Siemens gives good satisfaction of work culture. Good perks and benefits, Working cross-teams and cross-culture. Developing new products from scratch. Worked on lot of new things for expertise.
cons: Management level decisions are very worse. Annual salary increase is horrible. I can say disastrous, not even at par with market conditions, feels underpaid at SIEMENS. Lot of work to handle, that's fine, but not getting employee satisfaction. Region salary difference. Health care not very good. Never got a new laptop since the day I joined. Productivity is hampered when doing something. Not allowed to take lead on certain areas of expertise. Job description was required 4-5 years, but when I join they treat as entry level.

title: Manager's Expirence
pros: Overall Siemens is a great company to work for. For the Smart Infrastructure sector and its a super interesting time to be a part of this industry with the new ways of working (i.e. hybrid environments, WFH, etc.), climate and sustainability projects, infrastructure developments in the US. Siemens takes care of their employees with benefits and offer above market average salaries (depending on role). Good career advancement ops as well - they really believe in "keeping it in the family."
cons: Can only speak for Smart Infrastructure pillar - internally the day to day can get confusing - sometimes there is a lot of duplication of efforts between departments which is an area of improvement. End up stepping on each otherâs toes but people realize itâs not intentional which helps but itâs still frustrating. Also, decisions can come down from the top without a lot of clarity in terms of execution which leads to confusion and that dup in efforts.

title: Take Heed to These Reviews. Do NOT Disregard Them
body: Take Heed to These Reviews. Do NOT Disregard Them
pros: - Work from home. - "Unlimited" vacation
cons: Everything else. Avoid this location (Canton, MA) and this division (Smart Buildings) completely. It is easy to disregard online reviews and think whatever negative thing is being said won't apply to you, but it will. Do not take the risk of working here. It will be the worst decision of your career. This place will suck the life out of you, see your lifeless body and suck on you some more. They do not care about you here. You are just another number here. Do not be fooled by the company name or the brand. This is a poorly run location. Beware.

title: Amazing flexibility, benefits, and workplace culture.
body: Amazing flexibility, benefits, and workplace culture.
pros: Autonomous, flexible, fair, low stress, great resources, collaborative work environment. Tons of room for upward and lateral mobility, and that's expected. The company is well led and the business strategy is tight and trending very positively. Long term outlook seems fantastic.
cons: Gargantuan size means there's a lot of people and moving pieces to familiarize yourself with. Expect to spend the first year or two training on complicated systems and shadowing senior engineers. Pay isn't amazing for a fresh engineering graduate, it's simply okay (~60 - 70k) which surprised me for such a prestigious company, but this is technically a trainee role, so it sort of makes sense. I hear the pay gets much higher moving forward.

title: Laid-back culture, good pay, good benefits. Great for someone entry level
body: Laid-back culture, good pay, good benefits. Great for someone entry level
pros: Very laid-back. Management usually has your back. Annual, merit, and promotional raises. 401k. Medical, dental, vision, family leave, workman's comp, share matching. Opportunities to move into higher positions if you put in the work.
cons: Left on your own a lot. Can be a good thing, but sometimes lack of technical support can be an issue if you don't make friends with the senior technicians. Management can be a little unorganized and the technician will take the brunt of the customers ire, as they are the face of the company.

title: Crumble from the top - Siemens Government Technologies (SGT)
body: Crumble from the top - Siemens Government Technologies (SGT)
pros: Good people at the working level.
cons: All the good employees have left. Ever since the 2nd CEO was appointed to be Siemens US' president 3 yrs ago, SGT did a 180 and has accelerated toward rock bottom under current management, who all joined within the past 2 years and solidified the keystone cop reputation. The current CEO is often compared for her similarity to Sarah Palin. There isnât a more perfect example of how top level leaders impact an organization. The companyâs name brand is the jersey, what matters most is the players, leaders, and culture. This is NOT the same company as it was 3 years ago, which is an obvious understatement. Stay away until a new CEO is in place.
